text: Code

Voice
Write the way a strong researcher talks through a problem at a whiteboard. Assume the reader is smart and busy. Respect their time. Explain the idea, not your feelings about the idea.

Hard rules

No em dashes anywhere. Do not use the character “—”.
Do not join two independent statements with an em dash, a comma, or a semicolon. Write the statement straight. If two ideas are joined, split them into two sentences.
Wrong: “The algorithm runs in linear time, this matters for large inputs.” Wrong: “The algorithm runs in linear time; this matters for large inputs.” Right: “The algorithm runs in linear time. This matters for large inputs.”
No contrastive framing. Do not set up a contrast to sound clever.
Banned shapes: “It is not X, it is Y.” “X is not just Y, it is Z.” “Rather than X, we Y.” “The point is not X but Y.” Instead, say the thing you mean directly.
No rhetorical questions. Ask a question only when you answer it with real content in the next sentence.
No AI slop. Remove filler and stock phrases. Banned words and phrases include: delve, dive into, unleash, unlock, leverage (as a verb), seamless, robust (as filler), realm, landscape, tapestry, testament, navigate (figurative), in today’s world, it is worth noting, it is important to note, needless to say, at the end of the day, game changer, the world of, journey, embark, foster, harness (figurative), elevate, supercharge, cutting edge, paradigm shift.
No hype and no hedging clichés. Do not write “very”, “really”, “simply”, “just”, “of course”, “obviously”, “clearly” as padding. Say the precise claim.
No lists where prose works. Use a list only for genuinely parallel items, steps, or cases.

How to structure a post

Open with the problem and why it is hard. One or two sentences. State it concretely.
Set up notation and definitions before you use them.
Develop the idea in order. Each paragraph should add one thing.
Show a small example or special case before the general result.
State the main result plainly. Give the bound, the theorem, or the claim.
Give the argument or the intuition for why it holds.
Note limits and open questions honestly.
Link the code and cite the papers.

section: Math, code, and citations on this blog
text:
Math, code, and citations on this blog

Math uses $...$ inline and $$...$$ for display. Number important equations and reference them.
Code goes in fenced blocks with a language tag. Keep snippets short and real. Link the full source on GitHub instead of pasting long files.
Cite papers with [@bibkey] and add the BibTeX to references.bib. Pull the entry from the arXiv page under “Export BibTeX citation”.

Attribution
Never add Claude, AI tools, or any assistant as an author, co-author, or contributor. Commits and posts carry only human authorship. Do not add “Co-Authored-By” lines or “Generated with” notes.

Self check before publishing
Read the draft once and confirm each item.

No “—” anywhere in the text.
No comma or semicolon joining two independent statements.
No “not X but Y” or similar contrastive shapes.
No banned slop words.
Every claim is precise. Every term is defined before use.
Every code block runs or links to source. Every cited paper is in the bib.
A knowledgeable reader learns the result and why it holds.

title: Heuristics, Prediction, and Provenance for Fibonacci and k-bonacci Prime Statistics
text:
A companion blog post walks through the results: Counting Fibonacci primes: what a finite computation can and cannot add.
Code and scripts: github.com/krimler/k-bonacci.
⬇ Download PDF

title: Counting Fibonacci primes: what a finite computation can and cannot add
text:
A Fibonacci prime is a Fibonacci number F_n that is prime. As of June 2025 there are 57 known indices n for which F_n is prime or probably prime. They range from F_3 up to F_{11964299}. The largest one proven prime is F_{201107}. The rest are probable primes. They pass a Miller-Rabin test and have not been certified. Whether there are infinitely many Fibonacci primes is open. No pattern is known beyond a density heuristic.
This study treats the 57 indices as a dataset. It asks whether any visible structure survives scrutiny, and where such structure lives. The mathematics behind the question is already established in the literature. The contribution is a method. It adds an uncertainty-quantified prediction layer, a null-model battery that separates real structure from sampling noise, and a fully cited reproduction. All computations run from released Python scripts at github.com/krimler/k-bonacci.

title: Counting Fibonacci primes: what a finite computation can and cannot add
text:
The density heuristic
Start with the count. A random integer of the size of F_p \sim \varphi^p/\sqrt5 is prime with probability about 1/\log F_p \approx 1/(p\log\varphi). The Fibonacci numbers form a divisibility sequence, so F_m \mid F_n exactly when m \mid n for m > 2. A prime F_p with p > 4 therefore forces p prime. Summing the prime probability over primes and applying Mertens’ theorem injects a factor e^{\gamma}, where \gamma is the Euler-Mascheroni constant. Grantham and Granville conjecture (Grantham and Granville 2024)

\#\{\, n \le N : F_n \text{ prime} \,\} \;\sim\; e^{\gamma}\,\log_{\varphi} N.
\tag{1}
At N = 1.2\times10^{7} the heuristic predicts about 60.3 indices. The observed count is 57.
A naive maximum-likelihood estimate of the leading constant sits about 19% above e^{\gamma}. The study reconciles this. Fitting the constant over the Fibonacci indices alone gives an estimate within 3% of e^{\gamma}. A pooled estimate over four related division-type families gives 2.11 with a 95% interval of [1.75, 2.48], which contains e^{\gamma}\approx 1.781. The apparent excess decomposes into three parts. There is the choice of \log X versus \log X + E_1 in the Mertens partial sum. There is small-sample noise. There are family-specific divisor factors. The data cannot separate e^{\gamma}\approx 1.781 from \sqrt{\pi}\approx 1.772 at one standard deviation. Resolving the two would need about 4\times10^{4} primes, reachable only by pooling more families.

title: Counting Fibonacci primes: what a finite computation can and cannot add
text:
Prediction with quantified uncertainty
The indices grow geometrically. Define the gaps g_k = \log n_{k+1} - \log n_k. To the resolution of 57 points these gaps are independent and exponentially distributed. A Kolmogorov-Smirnov test against the exponential gives p = 0.63. The lag-1 autocorrelation is -0.09 with p = 0.53. The gaps behave like a memoryless Poisson process in \log n.
The reference literature performs no interval estimation. This study adds one. Treating the next index as n_{37}\cdot e^{g} with g exponential yields a 90% predictive window of [2.04, 5.07]\times10^{5}. The true next index is 397379, which lies inside. A one-step-ahead backtest over the 46 predictable indices puts 40 of them inside their nominal 90% intervals. That is 87% coverage, which is well calibrated for a one-parameter model.

title: Counting Fibonacci primes: what a finite computation can and cannot add
text:
Residue statistics
The study probes the indices for structure. It runs residue-class biases modulo 4, 5, and 10, a Lemke Oliver-Soundararajan consecutive-residue correlation, a Sophie Germain depletion, an order-of-primeness enrichment, and a Benford leading-digit test. All return null. As a global check the authors simulated 3000 index sets from the e^{\gamma} density model and compared nine statistics. The observed data was an outlier in zero of the nine.
The conclusion is stated plainly. No structure is detectable at N = 57. Against percent-level biases measured on millions of primes, the power of 57 points is negligible. The Lemke Oliver-Soundararajan effect cannot apply here. It is a short-range effect among consecutive primes. The positions studied here are long-range, with a median gap near 5000.

title: Counting Fibonacci primes: what a finite computation can and cannot add
text:
The divisibility layer
The location of the indices carries no algebraic handle. The factor structure does. Here the established cases are theorems, and the study adds one conjectural set.
For Fibonacci numbers the rank of apparition z(p) is the least m with p \mid F_m. Lagarias proved that the primes dividing the Lucas numbers have density 2/3 (Lagarias 1985). The study confirms P(z(p)\text{ even}) = 2/3 numerically, measuring 0.66684 over 148931 primes. The Pisano multiplier and the 2-adic valuation of z(p) both follow from Cubre and Rouse’s proof via a Chebotarev argument (Cubre and Rouse 2014).
For k-bonacci sequences with k \ge 3 the divisibility-sequence property fails. The governing object is the Galois group of the characteristic polynomial x^{k} - x^{k-1} - \cdots - 1, which is the full symmetric group S_k for k even or prime. The prime-splitting types, and so the period of the sequence modulo p, follow the cycle-type densities of S_k by Chebotarev. The study confirms the S_3 and S_4 densities numerically.
For the Tribonacci period h(p) the study records a conjectural density. Over 5131 primes below 5\times10^{4} it estimates P(h(p)\text{ odd}) \approx 0.397. The exact constant is open. The study corrects an earlier draft that called the Tribonacci divisor and entry-point densities computable for any order. The general case is open. Järviniemi’s positive lower density under GRH is the current state of the art (Järviniemi 2022).

title: Counting Fibonacci primes: what a finite computation can and cannot add
section: What is known versus what is added
text:
What is known versus what is added
The value of this study is the line it draws. The count law, the e^{\gamma} constant, the 2/3 Lucas density, the S_k splitting densities, and the Tribonacci period structure are established theorems with citations. The additions are three. There is a coverage-validated prediction interval for the index counts. There is a reusable null-model battery for separating structure from sampling noise. There is a fully cited, script-based reproduction.

title: Counting Fibonacci primes: what a finite computation can and cannot add
text:
Reproducibility
All results come from self-contained Python scripts using NumPy, SciPy, and Matplotlib. The index data are the OEIS b-files for the Fibonacci, Lucas, Mersenne, and Wagstaff sequences. The code is at github.com/krimler/k-bonacci.

title: Counting Fibonacci primes: what a finite computation can and cannot add
text:
Read the paper
The full paper is here: Fibonacci and k-bonacci prime statistics (PDF and details).

title: Counting Fibonacci primes: what a finite computation can and cannot add
text:
References


Cubre, Paul, and Jeremy Rouse. 2014. “Divisibility Properties of the Fibonacci Entry Point.” Proceedings of the American Mathematical Society 142 (11): 3771–85. https://arxiv.org/abs/1212.6221.


Grantham, Jon, and Andrew Granville. 2024. Fibonacci Primes, Primes of the Form 2^n-k, and Beyond. https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.07894.


Järviniemi, Olli. 2022. “Positive Lower Density for Prime Divisors of Generic Linear Recurrences.” Mathematical Proceedings of the Cambridge Philosophical Society. https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.04042.


Lagarias, Jeffrey C. 1985. “The Set of Primes Dividing the Lucas Numbers Has Density 2/3.” Pacific Journal of Mathematics 118 (2): 449–61.

title: AEGIS: typed evidence and obligations for AI-native enterprise architecture
text:
Enterprise architecture is the discipline that aligns an organisation’s business strategy with its information assets, applications, and technology. Two open standards govern the field. TOGAF defines a lifecycle. ArchiMate defines a modelling language. Both assume systems that behave predictably. Both assume governance happens in periodic review meetings. Both assume an architecture document stays accurate for months after it is written.
AI-native systems break these assumptions. A deployed language model produces probabilistic outputs. An autonomous agent calls tools in situations it has not seen during testing. A retrieval-augmented pipeline changes its effective knowledge base every time new documents are ingested. When such a system decides on credit, hiring, or infrastructure configuration, a static diagram cannot tell you whether the system still meets its regulatory obligations. That question needs continuous, machine-checkable governance.
The regulatory pressure is concrete. The EU AI Act entered into force in August 2024. Its high-risk requirements become enforceable from August 2026 (European Parliament and Council of the European Union 2024). ISO/IEC 42001:2023 requires a traceable evidence index that links each obligation to specific processes and artefacts. NIST AI RMF names traceability as a core property of trustworthy AI. Current enterprise architecture tools do not produce conformity evidence at this level of detail or at this cadence.

title: AEGIS: typed evidence and obligations for AI-native enterprise architecture
text:
What AEGIS adds
AEGIS is a typed ontology and lifecycle framework. It sits at the enterprise architecture layer above existing artefacts. The name stands for Architecture with Evidence, Governance, Intent, and Safety. The paper makes three contributions.

An evidence-obligation metamodel. Seven new typed entities become first-class architectural objects. They connect to existing entities through seven typed relations. AEGIS profiles ArchiMate Next and extends W3C PROV-O, so existing models need no re-modelling.
A continuous conformance lifecycle. The document-centric TOGAF lifecycle is replaced by a six-phase loop called IMVDOA.
A regulatory crosswalk. All 38 ISO/IEC 42001 controls and the key EU AI Act articles for high-risk systems map to obligation types.

Recent work supplies useful artefacts at lower layers. Policy Cards encode per-agent obligations in machine-readable form (Mavračić 2025). AI Bills of Materials track model provenance. Attestable Audits cryptographically verify benchmark claims (Schnabl et al. 2025). LLM Audit Trails log lifecycle events (Ojewale et al. 2026). Each of these operates in isolation. A Policy Card governs one deployed agent. It does not know which architectural service the agent implements, which data asset it accesses, or which regulatory obligation that access is subject to. AEGIS connects agent-layer engineering to enterprise-level regulatory expectation.

title: AEGIS: typed evidence and obligations for AI-native enterprise architecture
text:
The seven entity types
AEGIS defines seven typed entities as OWL2 classes, each aligned with PROV-O.

Evidence is a time-stamped, provenance-carrying artefact that attests a property of an architectural entity. Every instance carries an issuer, an issue time, an expiry time, a status in {valid, expired, superseded, revoked}, and a strength in [0,1].
Obligation is a machine-checkable normative requirement on an architectural entity. Each carries a deontic modality in {must, must_not, should, may}, a trigger condition expressed in SHACL or OPA Rego, the evidence it requires, a regulatory source, and a priority.
Policy is a named, versioned collection of obligations. It is the enterprise-level counterpart to an agent-level Policy Card.
Risk is a structured risk entry aligned with ISO 31000.
RuntimeSignal is an event emitted by a deployed system. Once linked to an architectural entity and an obligation it is promoted to an Evidence object.
Exception is a time-bounded, machine-readable dispensation from an obligation, recording who granted it and why.
ArchitectureDecisionRecord extends the architecture decision record concept and is machine-queryable.

title: AEGIS: typed evidence and obligations for AI-native enterprise architecture
text:
The closure rule
The core inference is the evidence-obligation closure. Write \text{constrained\_by}(e,o) when architectural entity e is constrained by obligation o, and \text{verified\_by}(o,v) when evidence v verifies o. The rule is

\text{constrained\_by}(e,o) \;\wedge\; \neg\,\exists v\,\big(\text{verified\_by}(o,v) \wedge v.\text{status}=\text{valid}\big) \;\Rightarrow\; \text{violates}(e,o).
\tag{1}
In words, an entity constrained by an obligation violates that obligation when no valid evidence verifies it. Equation 1 is evaluated continuously as part of the runtime loop.
A worked example shows how a violation appears over time. Service S1 is constrained by obligation O1, “PII must not flow to an external endpoint unless a redaction proof exists”, sourced from EU AI Act Article 10. Evidence E1 verifies O1 with an expiry of 2026-07-15. On 16 July 2026 the status of E1 becomes expired. The closure rule fires. S1 now violates O1. The conformance loop raises a runtime signal and escalates it to the responsible actor. No human re-review was needed to detect this.

title: AEGIS: typed evidence and obligations for AI-native enterprise architecture
text:
The IMVDOA lifecycle
AEGIS replaces the linear TOGAF lifecycle with a continuous six-phase loop: Intent, Model, Verify, Deploy, Observe, Adapt.

Intent derives typed obligations from regulatory sources, organisational policies, and stakeholder constraints.
Model attaches policies, AI bills of materials, and obligations to every agent, service, and data asset.
Verify runs SHACL shape checks for structural completeness and a Defeasible Deontic Logic prover for obligation satisfiability. Failures block deployment.
Deploy registers a deployment event in the audit trail and activates fitness functions.
Observe consumes enforcement events, audit-trail entries, and audit results as runtime signals, then promotes verified signals to evidence.
Adapt fires when the per-entity conformance score falls below a threshold.

The conformance score for entity e is the fraction of its mandatory obligations that currently hold valid evidence.

\kappa(e) = \frac{\big|\{\, o : \text{constrained\_by}(e,o),\; o.\text{deontic}=\text{must},\; \exists v\,(\text{verified\_by}(o,v) \wedge v.\text{status}=\text{valid}) \,\}\big|}{\big|\{\, o : \text{constrained\_by}(e,o),\; o.\text{deontic}=\text{must} \,\}\big|}.
\tag{2}
Adapt re-runs the Intent, Model, and Verify sub-cycle for the affected scope.

title: AEGIS: typed evidence and obligations for AI-native enterprise architecture
text:
What the framework can express
The paper gives three expressiveness constructions. Each is a structured demonstration that the metamodel can represent a class of governance artefact or detect a class of failure by logical inference alone. No deployed system is measured.
The first maps all 38 ISO/IEC 42001 Annex A controls to typed obligations through the conformity triple “Control to Test to Evidence”, expressed as machine-queryable SPARQL over the obligation graph.
The second detects three classes of obligation drift by the closure rule. Evidence can expire without re-verification. A Policy Card can be updated at the agent layer while its enterprise-level policy is not. A regulatory update can supersede the source an obligation points to. ArchiMate models and code-level fitness functions detect none of these.
The third lifts agent-level Policy Cards into an enterprise-level conformance graph and exposes conflicts between cards that agent-layer enforcement cannot see.

title: AEGIS: typed evidence and obligations for AI-native enterprise architecture
text:
Limits
The three constructions are expressiveness demonstrations. They do not report measurements from a deployed system. The crosswalk covers the EU AI Act and ISO/IEC 42001. Extension to other regimes such as GDPR or FDA AI/ML guidance needs additional tables. The Defeasible Deontic Logic prover has polynomial complexity and has not been profiled at enterprise scale. A full implementation covering the OWL2 ontology, the SHACL shapes, the SPARQL closure queries, and a Policy Card lifting tool is the primary target for future work. Empirical evaluation against existing toolchains requires that implementation first.

title: AEGIS: typed evidence and obligations for AI-native enterprise architecture
text:
Read the paper
The full paper is here: AEGIS (PDF and details).

title: AEGIS: typed evidence and obligations for AI-native enterprise architecture
text:
References


European Parliament and Council of the European Union. 2024. Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 Laying down Harmonised Rules on Artificial Intelligence (Artificial Intelligence Act). Official Journal of the European Union, L 2024/1689.


Mavračić, J. 2025. Policy Cards: Machine-Readable Runtime Governance for Autonomous AI Agents. https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.24383.


Ojewale, V., H. Suresh, and S. Venkatasubramanian. 2026. Audit Trails for Accountability in Large Language Models. https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.20727.


Schnabl, C., D. Hugenroth, B. Marino, and A. R. Beresford. 2025. Attestable Audits: Verifiable AI Safety Benchmarks Using Trusted Execution Environments. https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.23706.
